rulings.Case Title: HADAS GERGIS VS. PAT LEARY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Dismiss For Failure To Try Case Within Five Years And Six Months; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery calendar for Tuesday, March 4, 2025, Line 1. RESPONDENTS PAT LEARY, IN HER OFFICIAL CAPACITY AS ACTING DIRECTOR OF THE CALIFORNIA DEPARTMENT OF SOCIAL SERVICES, AND CALIFORNIA DEPARTMENT OF SOCIAL SERVICES' Motion To Dismiss For Failure To Try Case Within Five Years And Six Months. Hearing required to set future dates. The court's tentative appears below. Respondent California Department of Social Services (CDSS)'s motion to dismiss petitioner Hadas Gergis' petition for writ of mandate is denied. (The Court's complete tentative ruling has been emailed to the parties.) For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: QIAN & NEMECEK LLP VS. ROCK CLAPPER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: PLAINTIFF QIAN & NEMECEK LLP'S MOTION FOR ISSUANCE OF BENCH WARRANT AND FOR MONETARY SANCTIONS ( ADDED FOR POSTING OF PART 2 OF 2 OF THE TENTATIVE RULING)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery calendar for Tuesday, March 4, 2025, Line 7 (Part 2 of 2 of the tentative ruling). Plaintiff's Motion For Issuance Of Bench Warrant And For Monetary Sanctions. An evidentiary showing must be made that the contemnor can actually perform the act he is to be imprisoned for refusing to perform. (Noorthoek v. Superior Court of San Luis Obispo County (1969) 269 Cal.App.2d 600, 609 ["In order to support an indefinite commitment under section 1219, there must be a finding, based upon evidence, that the contemner has the present ability." [citation omitted].) The court's order of October 25 found Clapper in contempt, but did not pronounce a punishment and did not make a finding of present ability to comply. This court is aware of no authority that would allow it to set a punishment some months later, even pursuant to section 1219(a). Plaintiff also seeks discovery sanctions in the amount of $13,107.18 for the fees incurred in bringing this motion. Sanctions are available against one who makes or opposes a discovery motion without substantial justification, but this is not a motion brought under the discovery statutes and Plaintiff has not succeeded in its primary goal of obtaining a bench warrant. The court does not award sanctions payable to Plaintiff. The court does, however, order Clapper and NGB Markets (each of them) to pay $1500 to the San Francisco Superior Court as a sanction for disobeying the court's order compelling responses to the discovery, issued on September 17, 2025, pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 177.5. The circumstance justifying the order is that the court issued, on September 17, 2025, an order compelling Clapper and NGB to comply with the court's previous discovery order. Plaintiff has made an evidentiary showing, supported by declarations, that neither Clapper nor NGB has yet complied with this court's orders dated February 15, 2023 (ordering responses to post-judgment discovery) and September 17, 2025 (compelling compliance with post-judgment discovery order). Although Plaintiff brought a contempt motion against Clapper, as discussed above, that motion did not result in imposition of any sanction. Moreover, Plaintiff's moving papers demonstrate that the disobedience to the court order is ongoing, because Clapper and NGB have not yet complied with the order compelling their responses to discovery. Sanctions are due to the court within 10 days of notice of entry of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: QIAN & NEMECEK LLP VS. ROCK CLAPPER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Issuance Of Bench Warrant And For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery calendar for Tuesday, March 4, 2025, Line 7 (Part 1 of 2 of the tentative ruling). PLAINTIFF QIAN & NEMECEK LLP's Motion For Issuance Of Bench Warrant And For Monetary Sanctions. Plaintiff's unopposed motion for issuance of bench warrant and for monetary sanctions is denied in part and granted in part. Plaintiff has established, through declarations, that defendant Rock Clapper has willfully violated the court's post-judgment orders to provide responses to requests for production and interrogatories. Plaintiff now seeks a bench warrant for Clapper's arrest, along with monetary sanctions payable to Plaintiff as a discovery sanction, and $1500 sanctions payable to the court for each defendant pursuant to Penal Code 177.5. It should come as no surprise that courts strictly construe, and follow to the letter, statutes providing for the arrest of a person. The court denies the request for a bench warrant that is before it for two reasons. First, a bench warrant (as distinct from other kinds of warrants that Plaintiff has not sought with this noticed motion) may only issue in prescribed instances. Penal Code section 978.5, the statutory source of a court's authority to issue a bench warrant, provides: "(a) A bench warrant of arrest may be issued when a defendant fails to appear in court as required by law, including, but not limited to, the following situations: (1) If the defendant is ordered by a judge or magistrate to personally appear in court at a specific time and place. (2) If the defendant is released from custody on bail and is ordered by a judge or magistrate, or other person authorized to accept bail, to personally appear in court at a specific time and place." There are additional subsections, but each of them only applies in the context of a criminal case where a defendant has been ordered by the court to appear at a particular time and place. That is not the case here. Plaintiff also points to Penal Code section 166 as a source of judicial authority to issue a bench warrant. That section defines the crime of failing to follow a court order, but a judge cannot initiate prosecution under the action. Judges do not initiate criminal proceedings that might give rise to an arrest warrant (and a subsequent bench warrant); only the executive branch has that authority. Plaintiff offers no other authority for the court to issue a bench warrant. Second, the contempt order issued by the court on October 25, 2024 is not an independent source of authority to issue a bench warrant. Contempt proceedings are analogous to a mini-trial. A court can summarily punish contempt that happens in its presence, or it can issue notice and have an evidentiary hearing about contempt that occurs outside its presence. (Penal Code section 1211.) The alleged contemnor must be present at the hearing, but a warrant of commitment can issue to procure the alleged contemnor's presence for contempt proceedings. (Id. section 1212-1215.) An evidentiary hearing on the contempt occurs (section 1217), and then a determination of guilt is made (section 1218). If the person is found guilty of contempt, a sentence is pronounced: "a fine may be imposed on the person not exceeding one thousand dollars . . . payable to the court, or the person may be imprisoned not exceeding five days, or both." (Id. section 1218(a).) However, if the contempt is for failing to perform an act that the contemnor can perform, the contemnor may be imprisoned until he performs the act. (Id. section 1219(a).) (End of Part 1 of 2 of the tentative ruling) =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: THOMAS A. KARAS ET AL VS. ANDREAS ANGELOPOULOS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside Order Granting Plaintiffs Motion To Compel Defendant Andreas Angelopoulos Appearance At Deposition And To Deem Requests For Admission Admitted And Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Tuesday, March 4, 2025, Line 9. DEFENDANT ANDREAS ANGELOPOULOS TRUSTEE OF THE ANDREAS ANGELOPOULOS AND POLLY ANGELOPOULOS REVOCABLE LIVING TRUST's Motion To Set Aside Order Granting Plaintiffs Motion To Compel Defendant Andreas Angelopoulos Appearance At Deposition And To Deem Requests For Admission Admitted And Monetary Sanctions. The court denies Defendant Andreas Angelopoulos's motion to set aside this court's order granting Plaintiff Thomas Karas's motion to compel Defendant's appearance at deposition, to deem RFAs admitted, and for monetary sanctions. Angelopoulos seeks relief under CCP 473 and contends he did not receive notice of the motion or hearing. Angelopoulos has not carried his burden of showing excusable neglect. A party in a designated case (including civil cases like this one) are required to use e-filing. Service may be made through the e-filing system. (Local Rule 2.11(Q).) Although Angelopoulos complains that his paralegal's email address was the only one used to effect service, this is the email address that was used in his prior e-filings, and appears to be his designated e-filing address for this case. The service he complains of was made through the e-filing system. Angelopoulos has not shown with affirmative evidence or analysis in his moving papers that this email address was incorrect under the Code of Civil Procedure, the Rules of Court, or the Local Rules applicable to electronic service of moving papers, and if in fact his paralegal's email address is the designated e-filing address for this case, then service was proper and Angelopoulos's mistake was unreasonable. Nothing in this order precludes Angelopoulos from seeking new relief based on changed circumstances or new facts related to his health, but the parties should meet and confer first about the necessity for any such relief. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: HOUSING AUTHORITY OF THE CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN VS. NAN MCKAY & ASSOCIATES, INC. A CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatories
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Tuesday, March 4, 2025, Line 10. (Part 1 of 2 of the tentative ruling). CROSS DEFENDANT HOUSING AUTHORITY OF THE CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatories. Moving party Housing Authority of the City and County of San Francisco seeks an order compelling further responses to its Special Interrogatory Nos. 19 and 21. Special Interrogatory 19 seeks "all facts in detail" that support a particular contention responding party Nan McKay & Associates, Inc. has made in the litigation. Nan McKay responded with objections and then provided two examples, described in general terms as a pattern rather than a description of specific occasions, of facts supporting its contention. The court orders Nan McKay to supplement its response. Its objections lack merit; a party is entitled through the use of interrogatories to learn the facts on which an opponent bases its legal contentions. (CCP 2030.010(b).) Nan McKay's use of two generalized examples, rather than all specific facts on which its contentions are based, does not comply with its obligation to provide "as complete and straightforward" a response "as the information reasonably available to the responding party permits." (Id. 2030.220(a).) If Nan McKay does not yet have full information to make a complete response (such as because it is awaiting documents in discovery), it must nonetheless answer fully to the best of its present ability. (Id. 2030.220(b).) Nan McKay's objection that the interrogatory mistakenly refers to its response to RFA 9 is unpersuasive; the Housing Authority quoted the contention for which it seeks Nan McKay's supporting facts and thus is intelligible. Special Interrogatory 21 seeks a description of all "standard operating procedures" that Nan McKay claims the Housing Authority required it to exceed. Nan McKay responded by first referring to Contract No. 19-0014, contending that it identifies the procedures, and second by giving examples of standard procedures for HAP reconciliations that the Housing Authority asked it to exceed. The court finds that Nan McKay's invocation of 2030.230 is proper-it may refer to the contract in its response-but nonetheless incomplete. The question was not what SOPs applied to Nan McKay, which might be supplied by the contract, but which SOPs Nan McKay was asked by the Housing Authority to exceed. Unless the answer is "all of the SOPs contained in the contract," Nan McKay's reference to the contract's procedural requirements does not answer the question. Nan McKay shall supplement its response by specifying which procedural requirements of the contract it was asked or required by the Housing Authority to exceed. Nan McKay shall provide supplemental answers within 15 days of entry of this order in light of the parties' impending trial date. No party seeks sanctions. The court shall nonetheless order sanctions payable by the unsuccessful party unless it finds that the unsuccessful party acted with substantial justification. The court finds that both parties acted with substantial justification and does not award sanctions. (End of part 1 of 2 of the tentative ruling). =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: GRETCHEN PALMER VS. API MARKET, INC., ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Vacate July 10, 2024 Order & Lift Stay; Mem Of Points & Authorities In Support
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery calendar for Tuesday, March 4, 2025, Line 4. PLAINTIFF GRETCHEN PALMER's Motion To Vacate July 10, 2024 Order & Lift Stay. Plaintiff Gretchen Palmer's unopposed motion to lift the arbitration stay in this case is granted. The evidence before the court establishes that, after the court's July 10, 2024 order staying the matter in favor of arbitration pursuant to the parties' agreement, defendant API Market Inc. did not pay its retainer payment to the arbitrator. (Lebowitz Dec. paras. 5-6.) The private arbitration cannot proceed until API Market makes payment. (Ex. C to Lebowitz Dec.) The arbitration bill was due more than 30 days before Palmer sought lifting of the stay. (Ex. B to Lebowitz Dec.) On these facts, API Market is in material breach of the parties' arbitration agreement and has waived its right to compel Palmer to proceed with the arbitration. (CCP section 1281.98(a)(1).) Palmer may elect to return the matter to court (id. section 1281.98(b)(1)) and has done so here. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: MITCHELL D. BOULWARE VS. ABC TILE, INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery calendar for Tuesday, March 4, 2025, Line 6. 2 - DEFENDANT LOWE'S HOME CENTERS, LLC's MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT. Defendant Lowe's Home Centers, LLC seeks an order striking from Plaintiff's complaint his statement that he may seek relief for other stone slab products that caused his injury in the course of discovery. The court denies Lowe's motion to strike as to these allegations. Plaintiff's allegations are proper under Bockrath v. Aldrich Chemical Co., Inc. (1999) 21 Cal.4th 71, 80-81, which approved similar language. Lowe's attempts to distinguish Bockrath on the grounds that that case involved manufacturers and not a retailer like Lowe's, but that distinction is immaterial for purposes of the pleading standard. (The Court's complete tentative ruling has been emailed to the parties.) For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely.
